title: V.com weekend vote: Would you consider having a violin with unconventional design? - Violinist.com
content: We have thousands of human-written stories, discussions, interviews and reviews from today through the past 20+ years. Find them here: March 22, 2026, 2:12 PM · After speaking with violin maker Andrew Carruthers last week about some of his experimental violins with unusual design features, I started thinking philosophically about various "unusual" violins I have encountered over the years. The traditional design of a violin is a model of symmetry, acoustical physics and beauty that has been going strong for about 400 years. It's hard to argue with that! But as with any other art form, violin-making does continue to evolve, especially with the increased popularity of models that include 5 strings and more, electrical models that have different physical considerations than acoustic models, etc. My question to you: If all things were reasonably equal - the price, the sound, the functionality - would you consider playing on a violin that sported an unusual design or "look"? Here are a few examples that I have encountered through the years, which happen to be acoustical instruments: On the left is the "Pellegrina" viola, by David L. Rivinus. Its lopsided body gives off Salvador Dali vibes, but it's not just for aesthetics; it's part of an ergonomic design that allows for those long sound waves to have their space in the viola body, while shortening certain key distances to go easier on the human body. In the middle is Andrew Carruthers' X-Fiddle (2020), a design inspired by vibrational patterns in the wood plates (Chladni patterns). And on the left is Guy Rabut's "Black Violin," in which the New York-based luthier started with a del Gesù pattern and then redesigned aspects of the violin that had no primary acoustical function with the aim of creating a "bold modern visual presence." Of course, violin makers through the ages have experimented with design through the ages, creating scrolls with humans heads, elaborate designs and artwork on the body of the violin etc. And electric violins, with their different considerations for sound production, can be quite different (for example Mark Wood's Viper violins, or the Yamaha Electric Violin). So my question for you: Would you consider playing on a violin, viola or cello that sported an unusual design or "look"? Do you perhaps already have an instrument with an unconventional feature (an unusual scroll or design, a very different-looking electric, etc.) And please share your thoughts in the comments on violin design. What fits your aesthetic? What do you love about the traditional design? What would you enjoy (or tolerate) in an alternate design? March 22, 2026 at 10:30 PM · I understand getting a viola in an unconventional shape, the size is an obstacle for some people.   I just met a nice violist who plays an Izuka? Viola and it sounded great and was on the smaller side. March 22, 2026 at 10:37 PM · I remember a 'Strings' magazine article from many moons ago that featured the Rivinus and Rabut models. I'll see if I can dig it out, to add the date here. I thought both instruments were very beautiful. I think I spotted one of Rivinus' violas in the San Francisco Symphony when I heard them on tour. March 23, 2026 at 12:33 AM · Is my walnut and spruce violin unconventional? March 23, 2026 at 05:27 AM · I hardly ever play a conventional violin! March 23, 2026 at 06:52 AM · I would like to respectfully point out that the violin is not entirely symmetrical. As in nature, true symmetry is not useful, and the violin, having four strings of different diameter, cannot be an exception. The pegs are staggered; the base bar is on one side and the sound post on the other; the neck is canted to the right; even the bridge has a curve which elevates the strings more on one side than the other. I believe it was Oscar Wilde who stated that true beauty must include assymetry, but anyone feel free to correct me on this attribution! March 23, 2026 at 07:39 AM · I've seen and heard a Stroh fiddle; I've got a book on making violins and violas that are triangle-shaped; I love the Hardanger fiddle and would dearly love to have my own. If I came across a violin or viola or cello with a shape like the Pellegrina viola, I'd have to hear and play it before considering it. March 23, 2026 at 09:59 AM · Surely it doesn't matter what it looks like as long as it plays well and sounds good? March 23, 2026 at 02:01 PM · My main violin is purple. It's the first five string model I built. It's pretty unconventional in a lot of ways but from afar it just looks like a small viola with a dark varnish. It gets a lot of compliments.. and suspicious glances. I've made better sounding (not purple) instruments since then but I guess I just got attached to the thing. March 23, 2026 at 04:48 PM · Like a lot of people I voted for 1 because the most important thing is the sound. I could easily have voted 3, but it would still depend on the sound. March 23, 2026 at 04:58 PM · Yes I would definitely jump at the opportunity to play an instrument with decorative art work, if it was done tastefully like Andrea and Nicolò Amati or Pietro Guarneri of Mantua did with their creative inlays on certain instruments they made.  The post modernist designs that change the shape of the body or sound holes aren’t my cup of tea. March 24, 2026 at 11:48 AM · The article I mentioned above appeared in 'Strings' 10th Anniversary issue, July/August 1996. It featured instruments by Guy Rabut, David Rivinus, Christophe Landon, Suzy Norris and Danny Ferrington. Here's a report on a conversation with David Rivinus on the development of the Pellegrina viola: https://stringsmagazine.com/luthier-david-rivinus-fights-injuries-with-unorthodox-instruments/ You must be registered and logged in to submit a comment. Dimitri Musafia, Master Maker of Violin and Viola Cases
